mnemonics.title: FIXED VS VARIABLE PARTITIONS
mnemonics.content: FIXED: INTERNAL PROBLEM - FIXED SIZE CAUSES INTERNAL FRAGMENTATION. VARIABLE: EXTERNAL PROBLEM - VARIABLE SIZE CAUSES EXTERNAL FRAGMENTATION. REMEMBER: FIXED INSIDE WASTE, VARIABLE OUTSIDE WASTE.

mnemonics.title: MEMORY ALLOCATION STRATEGIES
mnemonics.content: FIRST FIND ANY SUITABLE - FIRST FIT. BEST BITS BUY SMALL - BEST FIT. WORST WANTS WIDE SPACES - WORST FIT. NEXT NURTURURES CONTINUITY - NEXT FIT.

mnemonics.title: FRAGMENTATION TYPES
mnemonics.content: INTERNAL: INSIDE THE PARTITION (FIXED). EXTERNAL: EXISTS OUTSIDE (SCATTERED). REMEMBER: I FOR INSIDE, E FOR EXTERNAL.

acronyms.expansion: First In First Out - Not a contiguous allocation strategy but used in page replacement
acronyms.usage: Confusing with First Fit, remember F-FIT searches first hole, FIFO is for page replacement

acronyms.expansion: Memory Management Unit - Hardware that handles address translation using base/limit registers
acronyms.usage: The MMU uses base register for relocation and checks against limit register for protection

acronyms.expansion: Central Processing Unit - Checks memory addresses against bounds registers before allowing access
acronyms.usage: When CPU executes memory access instructions, it verifies addresses using bounds registers

visualTips.visualization: Draw memory as a rectangle divided into 3-4 horizontal strips (partitions). Label each with a size. Show how a small process in a large partition leaves unused space (internal fragmentation) as a shaded unused area within the strip.
Draw memory blocks as boxes. Show alternating filled and empty boxes. Visualize empty boxes scattered (holes) that cannot combine to fit a larger process even though total empty space is enough.

Show before: scattered filled blocks with small gaps between them. After: all filled blocks pushed together on left, all free space consolidated into one large block on right. Show arrows indicating movement of blocks.

Draw two register boxes: BASE contains starting address, LIMIT contains size. Show how any memory reference (logical address) is checked: logical address + base = physical address, and logical address < limit for validity.

analogies.analogy: Like assigned parking spots of fixed sizes in a mall parking lot. If you have a small motorcycle but get assigned a large car spot, the unused space is wasted (internal fragmentation). You cannot use spots meant for larger vehicles.

Like seats in a movie theater with scattered empty seats. If a group of 5 people wants to sit together but only isolated single seats are available throughout, the group cannot be seated even though 5 seats are free (fragmentation).

Like rearranging furniture in a room to consolidate all free space into one area. You move all items together so you have one large empty space instead of many small gaps (like moving all chairs to one side).

Like finding the smallest parking space that fits your car. You drive around looking for the tightest fit, which leaves more room for other cars but takes longer to find than just taking the first spot.
